denial_pattern_sources: appeal_closed_as_withdrawn: State Insurance Commissioner complaint logs (CA DOI 2022, NY DFS 2023)
superseded_guideline: AHIP 2022 Coverage Policy Review; documented in multiple Cigna litigation cases 2021–2023
denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) related to Severe OCD, treatment-resistant (F42.2).

After clinical review using Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletins (CPBs) and InterQual criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Peer-to-peer not completed within 5-business-day window. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for prior authorization determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Auth denied because peer-to-peer review was not requested in window.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS). If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination through our internal appeals process. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management
clinical_context:
The 69-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Severe OCD, treatment-resistant (F42.2). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) as the next appropriate step.

Scheduling line was down; three documented call attempts. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on missing peer to peer without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
